prior.title: Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety - Green Building Services for Existing Buildings
prior.summary: LADBS homepage providing access to green building services and resources for existing buildings in Los Angeles, including plan review, permitting, inspection, enforcement, and property records services.
prior.full_brief:
# Los Angeles EBEWE Program — Green Building Services for Existing Buildings

## Operations Profile

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document covers the City of Los Angeles Existing Buildings Energy and Water Efficiency (EBEWE) Program, codified as Chapter 97 of the Los Angeles Municipal Code (LAMC), administered by the Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety (LADBS). It is produced for workspaces operating commercial facilities, warehouses, or logistics infrastructure in Los Angeles — any building footprint that may cross the program's size thresholds.

The document synthesises the LAMC Chapter 97 codified text, the LADWP utility partner benchmarking requirements, the California Energy Commission (CEC) AB 802 statewide layer, and the open EBEWE compliance dataset updated May 2026. Four lenses apply: substantive compliance, competitive positioning, client conversation, and action.

Convention: all building size references are gross square footage (GSF). "The workspace" refers to the freight forwarding operation and any buildings it owns, occupies, or manages within Los Angeles city limits.

---

# 1. Operational Cost Baseline for the Region

MONITORING

The EBEWE program generates direct, recurring compliance costs for covered buildings in Los Angeles. The sourced cost mechanisms are:

**Annual registration fee.** FACT — The EBEWE program dataset confirms "owners of buildings subject to the Ordinance" are required "to annually register their building (includes pay fees)." *Source: Existing Buildings Energy & Water Efficiency (EBEWE) Program dataset, data.lacity.org / City of Los Angeles, last updated May 31, 2026. https://catalog.data.gov/dataset/existing-buildings-energy-water-efficiency-ebewe-program.

**Fee and surcharge structure codified.** FACT — LAMC Chapter 97 contains dedicated sections: "Section 9709 Noncompliance Fee," "Section 9710 Submittal Fees," and "Section 9711 Building and Safety Development Surcharge." *Source: UpCodes — LA City Building Code 2023, Chapter 97 (EBEWE), Los Angeles City Building Code 2023. https://up.codes/viewer/los_angeles/ca-building-code-2022/chapter/new_97/existing-buildings-energy-and-water-efficiency-program.

*Specific current dollar amounts for submittal fees and the noncompliance fee are not available from the source blocks provided as of June 2026.* [GAP — fee_amounts]

**General Plan Maintenance Surcharge increase.** FACT — "Effective June 9, 2026, the General Plan Maintenance Surcharge (GPMS), a.k.a. Planning General Plan Maintenance Surcharge, increases from 7% to 10% (CF09-0969-S4)." *Source: LADBS main page, City of Los Angeles, accessed June 2026. https://ladbs.org/services/green-building/existing-buildings.

Analytical inference: For workspaces that own or lease large commercial or logistics facilities in Los Angeles and trigger EBEWE coverage, the combination of annual registration fees, possible noncompliance fees, and the June 2026 GPMS increase creates a stacking compliance cost structure that merits line-item budgeting ahead of each annual June 1 reporting cycle.

**LADWP data-feed processing lead time.** FACT — "The Los Angeles Benchmarking Guide recommends submitting data request(s) no later than March 1st to allow sufficient processing time ahead of the annual June 1st deadline." Application processing at LADWP "may take 4-6 weeks." *Source: LADWP — Building Benchmarking, Los Angeles Department of Water and Power, accessed June 2026. https://www.ladwp.com/construction-services/building-benchmarking.

Operational implication: For workspaces initiating EBEWE benchmarking for the first time, the 4-to-6-week LADWP processing window means a March 1 submission target for utility data requests — not June 1 — is the effective operational deadline to avoid a late filing.

---

# 2. Feasibility of Specific Operational Choices

**ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager (ESPM) — free tool, mandatory platform.**

FACT — The ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager is defined in LAMC Chapter 97 as "The United States Environmental Protection Agency's (US EPA) online tool for measuring, tracking, and managing a building's energy, water and greenhouse gas emission data and to benchmark the performance of a building." *Source: UpCodes — LA City Building Code 2023, Chapter 97 (EBEWE). https://up.codes/viewer/los_angeles/ca-building-code-2022/chapter/new_97/existing-buildings-energy-and-water-efficiency-program.

FACT — LADWP describes ESPM as "a free online tool that allows you to track the energy and water use for the entire building, and comply with the City ordinance and State Legislation." *Source: LADWP — Building Benchmarking. https://www.ladwp.com/construction-services/building-benchmarking.

**Automatic utility data feed — available for LADWP customers.**

FACT — "LADWP has established an interface with ESPM that will enable you to benchmark your building's energy and water consumption." Once connected, "LADWP provides automatic recurring updates to your property profile in the ESPM." *Source: LADWP — Building Benchmarking. https://www.ladwp.com/construction-services/building-benchmarking.

Analytical inference: For workspaces with multiple metered buildings served by LADWP, the automatic data-feed architecture reduces the manual data-entry burden substantially compared to jurisdictions without utility ESPM integration — but the workspace must still initiate the one-time application and share meters in ESPM using LADWP's assigned Admin ID.

**Tenant systems — scoped out of building owner obligations.**

FACT — LAMC Chapter 97 defines BASE BUILDING SYSTEMS to exclude: "Systems or subsystems owned by a tenant or for which a tenant bears full maintenance responsibility, that are within the tenant's leased space and exclusively serve such leased space, and for which the tenant pays all the energy and water bills according to usage and demand as measured by a meter or sub-meter." *Source: UpCodes — LA City Building Code 2023, Chapter 97 (EBEWE). https://up.codes/viewer/los_angeles/ca-building-code-2022/chapter/new_97/existing-buildings-energy-and-water-efficiency-program.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether a workspace that is a freight forwarder operating as a tenant in a covered LA building — rather than the building owner — bears any direct EBEWE registration or reporting obligation, or whether those obligations fall exclusively on the building owner, requires counsel review against the lease and LAMC Chapter 97 definitions.

---

# 3. Cost Comparison Against Alternatives

**Benchmarking compliance vs. noncompliance fee exposure.**

FACT — LAMC Chapter 97, Section 9709, establishes a "Noncompliance Fee" as a dedicated code provision. *Source: UpCodes — LA City Building Code 2023, Chapter 97 (EBEWE). https://up.codes/viewer/los_angeles/ca-building-code-2022/chapter/new_97/existing-buildings-energy-and-water-efficiency-program.

*Specific dollar amount for the noncompliance fee is not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* [GAP — penalty_summary]

Analytical inference: Standard municipal benchmarking noncompliance fees in comparable jurisdictions run in the range of hundreds to low thousands of dollars per year per building, but the workspace should obtain the current LADBS fee schedule directly to size the actual exposure.

**CEC AB 802 statewide layer — separate but parallel.**

FACT — California AB 802 "requires the Energy Commission to establish a building energy use benchmarking and public disclosure program for certain buildings." FACT — The CEC program covers "buildings with more than 50,000 square feet of gross floor area." FACT — "Owners of commercial buildings with more than 50,000 square feet and no residential utility accounts must report annually starting June 1, 2018." *Source: California Energy Commission — Building Energy Benchmarking Program, CEC, accessed June 2026. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/building-energy-benchmarking-program.

Analytical inference: A workspace-owned or workspace-managed building above 50,000 GSF in Los Angeles faces dual compliance: EBEWE (city layer, annual LADBS registration + benchmarking report) and AB 802 (state layer, annual CEC report via ESPM by June 1). Both use ESPM as the submission platform, so data-entry effort is partially shared, but the filing obligations are separate. Buildings between 20,000 and 50,000 GSF face only the EBEWE city layer.

---

# 4. Cross-Regional Strategic Implications

**Coverage thresholds and cargo-vertical filtering.**

FACT — LAMC Chapter 97, Section 9703 (Scope), states the chapter applies to buildings "Privately owned and are 20,000 square feet or more" and to City-owned buildings "7,500 square feet or more." *Source: UpCodes — LA City Building Code 2023, Chapter 97 (EBEWE). https://up.codes/viewer/los_angeles/ca-building-code-2022/chapter/new_97/existing-buildings-energy-and-water-efficiency-program.

FACT — Chapter 97 explicitly excludes from scope "sound stages; structures primarily used for the production and post-production of motion pictures and television, and similar uses." *Source: UpCodes — LA City Building Code 2023, Chapter 97 (EBEWE). https://up.codes/viewer/los_angeles/ca-building-code-2022/chapter/new_97/existing-buildings-energy-and-water-efficiency-program.

Analytical inference: For workspaces in the freight forwarding sector operating cargo-handling warehouses, staging facilities, or office premises in Los Angeles at or above 20,000 GSF, the EBEWE program is likely triggered. The film/TV sound-stage carve-out is specific and does not extend to general logistics or warehousing facilities.

**Ordinance effective date and program vintage.**

FACT — "The City's Existing Buildings Energy & Water Efficiency (EBEWE) Program was established in 2016 (LA Municipal Code Section 91.9701, Ordinance No. 184674) and is administered by the Department of Building and Safety (LADBS)." *Source: EBEWE Program dataset, data.lacity.org / City of Los Angeles, last updated May 31, 2026. https://catalog.data.gov/dataset/existing-buildings-energy-water-efficiency-ebewe-program.

FACT — The LADWP benchmarking page states "The City of Los Angeles established the Existing Buildings Energy and Water Efficiency (EBEWE) ordinance in 2017 to encourage building owners to reduce energy and water consumption." *Source: LADWP — Building Benchmarking. https://www.ladwp.com/construction-services/building-benchmarking.

Analytical inference: The apparent date discrepancy (2016 in the dataset metadata per Ordinance No. 184674; 2017 in LADWP's description) likely reflects the difference between the ordinance adoption date and the effective enforcement date. The corroborating LADBS EBEWE page (listed in New Sources Identified below) states effective date 1/29/2017. The workspace should treat the program as fully operational and mature — this is not an emerging regulation but an enforced, multi-year compliance regime with an open public dataset tracking compliance status.

**Audit and retro-commissioning (A/RCx) cycle.**

FACT — LAMC Chapter 97 contains "Section 9706 Energy and Water Audits and Retro-Commissioning" as a dedicated requirement, and Chapter 97 defines both ENERGY AUDIT ("A systematic evaluation to identify potential modifications and improvements to a building's equipment and systems which utilize energy in order to optimize a building's overall energy performance") and RETRO-COMMISSIONING ("A systematic process for optimizing existing systems relating to building performance through the identification and correction of deficiencies in such systems"). *Source: UpCodes — LA City Building Code 2023, Chapter 97 (EBEWE). https://up.codes/viewer/los_angeles/ca-building-code-2022/chapter/new_97/existing-buildings-energy-and-water-efficiency-program.

*Specific cycle length, A/RCx trigger scores, and current phase deadlines applicable to each building cohort are not available from the source blocks provided as of June 2026.* [GAP — primary_deadline for A/RCx]

Analytical inference: The corroborating LADBS EBEWE page (listed below) describes a "5-year A/RCx cycle." Workspaces with covered buildings should confirm their cohort's current cycle position directly with LADBS, as missed A/RCx deadlines likely trigger the Section 9709 noncompliance fee.

---

# 5. Competitive Positioning in the Region

**Public dataset enables peer benchmarking.**

FACT — The EBEWE open dataset, updated May 31, 2026, "includes the Compliance Year, Building Address, Compliance Status, various energy and water use benchmark data, and the last 3 digits of the Assessor Identification Number (AIN)" and is publicly accessible. *Source: EBEWE Program dataset, data.lacity.org / City of Los Angeles, last updated May 31, 2026. https://catalog.data.gov/dataset/existing-buildings-energy-water-efficiency-ebewe-program.

Analytical inference: The public nature of EBEWE compliance data means that commercial clients, ESG-focused tenants, and cargo owners evaluating a freight forwarder's facility can look up the benchmarking compliance status and energy performance of any covered LA building. A workspace operating a non-compliant or high-EUI facility in Los Angeles carries reputational risk in client sustainability reviews.

**ENERGY STAR score as a commercial differentiator.**

FACT — ESPM generates "an ENERGY STAR® energy performance score between 1 and 100 for many eligible buildings." The score reflects "a measurement of a building's energy efficiency, normalized for a building's characteristics, operations, and weather." *Source: LADWP — Building Benchmarking. https://www.ladwp.com/construction-services/building-benchmarking.

Analytical inference: For workspaces competing for logistics or warehousing business with sustainability-minded cargo owners — particularly those tracking Scope 3 supply chain emissions — an ENERGY STAR score above 75 (the threshold for ENERGY STAR certification) represents a differentiated selling point. The workspace can reference the score directly from ESPM in RFP responses and client sustainability questionnaires without overclaiming.

No competitor-specific EBEWE compliance or energy performance data is cited here; the public dataset is the workspace's own tool for that research.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- "Our covered Los Angeles facilities are enrolled in the EBEWE program and submit annual benchmarking reports through ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager. Our compliance status and energy use intensity are publicly verifiable in the City's open dataset." (FACT-grounded; cite the EBEWE dataset.)
- "LADWP's automatic data feed means our utility consumption figures in the report are utility-certified, not self-estimated." (FACT-grounded; cite LADWP benchmarking page.)
- "California's AB 802 requires public disclosure of energy performance for buildings over 50,000 square feet — our LA facilities above that threshold are subject to both city and state reporting."

**What the workspace should not say:**

- Do not claim a specific ENERGY STAR score without pulling the current score from ESPM — scores change annually with updated data.
- Do not represent EBEWE compliance as equivalent to LEED certification or as evidence of a specific emissions reduction — the two are different frameworks.
- Do not assert that tenant-operated systems are excluded from the building's benchmarked energy footprint without confirming the metering and lease structure with counsel. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether separately metered tenant loads in a workspace-occupied building are excluded from the benchmarking report the owner must file depends on the specific metering arrangement and lease terms, and requires legal and technical review.

**Questions the workspace can pose to building landlords:**

- "Can you confirm the building's current EBEWE compliance status and last reported ENERGY STAR score?"
- "Is the LADWP automatic data feed active for this building, and has LADBS issued any noncompliance notices?"
- "Where is this building in the current 5-year A/RCx cycle, and is an audit or retro-commissioning study scheduled?"

---

# 7. Pending Changes That Shift the Calculus

MONITORING — Re-check window: Q4 2026

**CEC AB 802 regulatory language amendments — active rulemaking.**

FACT — The California Energy Commission held a "Workshop on Building Energy Benchmarking Regulatory Language Changes" on October 13, 2025, and opened Docket Log for 2025 Amendments (25-OIR-01). *Source: California Energy Commission — Building Energy Benchmarking Program. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/building-energy-benchmarking-program.

Analytical inference: The open 2025 amendments docket (25-OIR-01) at the CEC signals active rulemaking on the AB 802 statewide layer. Any changes adopted through that proceeding could shift the public disclosure threshold, reporting deadlines, or covered building definitions. Workspaces with LA buildings above 50,000 GSF should monitor the CEC docket for final regulatory language, expected to be published through the E-Filing system when it becomes available.

**CEC Benchmarking Reference Number (BRN) — now required.**

FACT — "Starting in 2023 and continuing each subsequent year, you must add a benchmarking reference number (BRN) to your report." *Source: California Energy Commission — Building Energy Benchmarking Program. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/building-energy-benchmarking-program.

Operational implication: Workspaces that obtained a LADBS Building ID (BID) for prior EBEWE filings and have not separately obtained a CEC Benchmarking Reference Number (BRN) are now out of step with the state layer requirement. Both identifiers are needed for full dual-layer compliance.

**GPMS surcharge increase — effective June 9, 2026.**

FACT — The General Plan Maintenance Surcharge increases from 7% to 10% effective June 9, 2026 (CF09-0969-S4). *Source: LADBS main page. https://ladbs.org/services/green-building/existing-buildings.

Operational implication: Any permit or fee-bearing LADBS submission on or after June 9, 2026 — including EBEWE-related filings — carries the higher 10% GPMS surcharge. For workspaces planning building upgrades or retrofits triggered by EBEWE A/RCx findings, this cost increase applies immediately.

---

---

| Source Name | URL | Tier Estimate | Why This Source Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| LADBS – Existing Buildings Energy & Water Efficiency (EBEWE) Program | https://dbs.lacity.gov/services/green-building-sustainability/ebewe | 2 | Primary LADBS program page establishing Division 97 LAMC authority, effective date 1/29/2017, two-phase benchmarking + A/RCx structure — fills the effective-date gap and confirms 5-year A/RCx cycle not fully captured in the UpCodes text block. |
| LADBS – Annual Benchmark Compliance Instructions (updated Feb 2026) | https://dbs.lacity.gov/sites/default/files/efs/forms/pc17/annual-benchmark-compliance-instructions-final.pdf | 2 | Official step-by-step compliance guide (last updated 02/18/2026); covers ESPM registration, LADWP/SoCalGas data feeds, and submission portal — operationally critical for workspaces initiating or updating compliance. |
